prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: There's something profound about Leonard Cohen's "Hallelujah" that never fails to resonate with me. What makes it so moving isn't just the melancholic beauty of the melody or the poetic imagery—it's how Cohen's slightly cracked, world-weary voice seems to carry the collective weight of human experience. The way he languidly teams "hallelujah" with tales of biblical downfall, romantic heartbreak, and spiritual yearning creates this unique tension between reverence and desolation. For me, it perfectly captures life's contradictions—our capacity for both ecstasy and despair, for faith and doubt—and delivers it with such aching tenderness that it somehow feels both ancient and intimately personal. Every time I hear it, I find myself breathing a little slower, as if momentarily connected to something larger than myself, yet deeply familiar.
